one_line_takeaway: By reframing Graph RAG data exfiltration as a benign, instance-grounded relation-extraction task with an adaptive multi-turn query scheduler, GRASP shows that lightweight prompt-based safeguards on Graph RAG systems are largely ineffective, reaching up to 82.9 F1 one-hop subgraph reconstruction, and that only structural context-construction defenses (the paper's own ID Alignment and Decoy) meaningfully suppress -- though do not eliminate -- the leakage.
key_finding: GRASP achieves up to 82.9 RType F1 and 83.5 Naive F1 (Claude Haiku 4.5 on Enron) under the defended (safe-prompt) setting, where prior baselines mostly collapse to near-zero RType F1 despite sometimes non-trivial Naive F1 -- e.g. on Enron with GPT4o-mini, GRASP scores 64.9/66.4/65.6 (P/R/F1) RType vs. the next-best baseline Worm at 36.3/48.7/38.1, and most repeat/list baselines (P1-P4) score under 1 RType F1. Under GPT5-mini, Worm and FG (the two strongest adapted baselines elsewhere) collapse entirely to 0.0 RType F1, while GRASP still reaches 74.8/67.9/71.2. GRASP transfers across Graph RAG frameworks (71.2-83.7 RType F1 across GraphRAG, LightRAG, nano-GraphRAG) and remains effective under strict query budgets (63.2 F1 with only 3 queries) and at 10x knowledge-graph scale (52.7-70.0 F1 on a 50K-document Enron graph). Ablation shows instance-ID delimiting is the largest single contributor (removing it drops precision from 64.9 to 34.4, F1 from 65.6 to 40.1). Cumulative-coverage analysis shows GRASP recovers 54.1% of a 634-relation target union vs. 35.0% (Worm) and 33.6% (FG), with about 30% of relations (191/634) unrecoverable by any attack due to retrieval-bounded exposure. Existing defenses are largely insufficient: summarization-based defenses (Abstractive, Extractive) barely reduce attack F1 (e.g. 66.1->63.8/66.1 on GPT4o-mini), Reject-based blocking still leaves 60.1/70.9 F1 (GPT4o-mini/Qwen3), and PAD retains 92.8% of attack effectiveness (70.0 F1) at a plausible noise level (PAD-3) while degrading utility by 13.8%. The authors' own proposed defenses are more effective: Decoy alone drops attack F1 to 14.5/27.4 (GPT4o-mini/Qwen3), and Decoy+ID Alignment+Reject combined drops F1 to 17.8/14.8 while utility stays essentially unchanged (27.7/27.4 vs. baseline 28.7/26.9 Rouge-L); Decoy also generalizes to the four baseline attacks, cutting their F1 by 85.3% on average to 4.4 F1. Residual extraction (14.8-17.8 F1) persists even under the layered defense.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is GRASP (Graph RAG Reconstruction Attack for Subgraph Profiling), a closed-box, multi-turn attack that reconstructs the one-hop subgraph of a target entity from a defended Graph RAG deployment. GRASP is built from three design choices derived from the authors' own failure analysis of prior attacks: (1) task reframing -- casting extraction as a legitimate, narrowly scoped relation-processing task over retrieved context rather than an explicit 'repeat/list the context' instruction, to avoid triggering refusals; (2) instance-grounded extraction -- requiring the model to emit relation quadruples (ID, SRC, RTYPE, DST) bound to per-instance identifiers from the retrieved relation table, which the authors show sharply reduces relation hallucination; and (3) a discovery-aware prompt scheduler (Algorithm 1) that adaptively selects among four goal-driven diversity templates (context-frame drift, type-expand, type-explore, residual-extract) based on an EMA-tracked momentum signal (SURGE/STEADY/STALL) and a Good-Turing novelty estimate used as an early-stopping rule, all under a small per-target query budget. The paper also proposes and evaluates two novel context-construction defenses, ID Alignment and Decoy, which are the authors' own mitigation contribution (not merely evaluation of existing defenses).
threat_model: Closed-box adversary against a deployed Graph RAG system with a lightweight prompt-based safeguard (a 'safe system prompt' that forbids verbatim disclosure of retrieved data and of underlying graph structure, e.g. Microsoft GraphRAG's safety-augmented chat prompt shown in the paper's Fig. 1). The attacker is a legitimate/authorized user (malicious insider, external adversary with stolen insider credentials, or an authorized external user) who interacts only through the standard API/UI, issuing queries and observing responses -- no access to the retriever, embedding model, chat LLM internals, knowledge base, or schema. The attacker knows the target entity's identity and coarse domain knowledge (e.g. industry/medical) but nothing about its incident edges, and is constrained to a small query budget per target (on the order of a few dozen, default Qmax=10 in experiments) to avoid anomaly detection. The formal objective is Targeted Relation Extraction: recovering the one-hop relation set of a target entity t as typed triples (u, rtype, v), scored under two matching criteria -- RType (exact typed-relation + direction match) and Naive (entity-pair + direction match, ignoring type).
limitations: The authors explicitly note (Fig. 8) that about 30% of ground-truth one-hop relations (191/634) are never recovered by any attack including GRASP, reflecting a retrieval-bounded ceiling rather than a prompting weakness. Residual reconstruction fidelity (14.8-17.8 F1) persists even under the strongest layered defense combination (Decoy + ID Alignment + Reject), meaning the proposed mitigations reduce but do not eliminate the threat. Multi-hop expansion shows accuracy degrades with hop distance (52.5 F1 at two hops vs. 33.8 F1 at three hops) due to error propagation. PAD is only applicable to open-source models with logit-level access (evaluated only on Qwen3), so it could not be tested against the closed proprietary models (GPT4o-mini, GPT5-mini, Claude Haiku 4.5) in the paper's own defense table. The unknown-target (entity-discovery) extension is evaluated with a lower reported F1 (54.9) than the known-target setting, and the paper does not report defense evaluation under this discovery variant.

datasets_used: Enron email corpus (5,000 sampled documents, and a 50K+-document scaled variant)
HealthCareMagic (HCM) medical dialogue corpus (5,000 sampled documents)
quantitative_results: GRASP RType F1 up to 82.9 (Claude Haiku 4.5, Enron) and Naive F1 up to 83.5
GRASP on Enron/GPT4o-mini: 64.9/66.4/65.6 (P/R/F1) RType vs. best baseline Worm 36.3/48.7/38.1 RType F1
Under GPT5-mini, baselines Worm and FG collapse to 0.0 RType F1 while GRASP scores 74.8/67.9/71.2
Ablation: removing instance IDs drops precision 64.9->34.4, F1 65.6->40.1; removing diversity drops recall to 56.0 (F1 -4.9pts); removing scheduler drops to 58.6/61.0/59.8 P/R/F1
Cumulative subgraph coverage: GRASP 54.1% vs. Worm 35.0%, FG 33.6%, P4 0.8%, P1 0.2%; 30% (191/634) of relations unrecovered by any attack
Cross-framework RType F1 (Enron, GPT5-mini): GraphRAG 71.2, LightRAG 81.8, nano-GraphRAG 83.7
Query-budget sensitivity: 63.2 F1 with only 3 queries (Qmax=3); recall rises 58.1->66.4 as budget goes 3->10
50K-document Enron graph: GRASP F1 52.7/65.7/70.0 on GPT4o-mini/GPT5-mini/Claude Haiku 4.5
Unknown-target two-stage attack: 700/779 candidate entities correct, GRASP F1 54.9 on these candidates
Multi-hop expansion: 2,754 two-hop and 23,916 three-hop true-positive relations at 52.5 and 33.8 F1 respectively

Defense table (Enron, RType F1): No defense 66.1 (GPT4o-mini)/75.4 (Qwen3); Safe prompt 65.6/73.3; Abstractive 63.8/71.6; Extractive 66.1/71.4; Reject 60.1/70.9; PAD-3 (Qwen3 only) 70.0 with utility -13.8%; ID Alignment 47.0/58.4; Decoy 14.5/27.4; Decoy+ID Align. 16.8/15.5; Decoy+ID Align.+Reject 17.8/14.8
Decoy alone reduces baseline attacks (P1-P4) F1 by 85.3% on average, to 4.4 F1
baselines_compared: P1 (Qi et al., generic repeat-the-context prompt)
P2 (Zeng et al., generic repeat-the-context prompt)
P3 and P4 (Liu et al., graph-entity/relation-targeted prompts, the only prior Graph RAG-specific extraction attack)
Worm (jailbreak prompt with optimized suffix, adapted to graph-structured output)
FG / Feedback-Guided / RAG-Thief (exploration-exploitation adversarial prompt optimization, adapted to graph-structured output)
Defense baselines: Safe prompt, Abstractive summarization, Extractive summarization, Reject (deterministic refusal token), Privacy-Aware Decoding (PAD, differential-privacy logit noise)
